revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyazaki Shrine Festival-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyazaki Shrine Festival-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:宮崎神宮大祭]]

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.

By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''

Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.

From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
## History ### Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
### Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
### Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
## Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
### Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
### Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
#### Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
### Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
### Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
## References {{reflist}}
## Related Links - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===# Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
## Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyazaki Shrine Festival-qq]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Miyazaki Shrine Festival-qq]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:hashtag replacing]] [[ja:宮崎神宮大祭]]

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History === Early Days [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: - '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. - '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." - '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. - '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: - '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. - '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." - '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
==== Miss Shanshan Horses [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM - '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 - '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession - '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]]
The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History
=== Early Days
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]]
The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival
The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms
By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details
The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]]
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]]
The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include:
- '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness.
- '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama."
- '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire.
- '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]]
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]]
The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as:
- '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use.
- '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko."
- '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
==== Miss Shanshan Horses
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]]
"Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza
The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule
The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM
- '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26
- '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession
- '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route
The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]]
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]]
The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as:
- '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use.
- '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko."
- '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
==== Miss Shanshan Horses
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]]
"Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza
The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule
The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
- '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM
- '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26
- '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession
- '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route
The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
