revision.comment: 23 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宮崎神宮大祭 up to rev 99676427
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|2008年の御神幸行列。御鳳輦がデパート前交差点を通る。]] '''[[宮崎神宮]]の[[例祭|大祭]]'''（みやざきじんぐうのたいさい）は[[10月26日]]に催行され、例祭後最初の[[土曜日]]・[[日曜日]]に御[[神幸祭]]が[[宮崎市]]内で行われる。
特に御神幸行列と神賑行列から成る御神幸祭は'''神武さま'''（祭神の[[神武天皇]]の意）と称され<ref>『宮崎県大百科事典』（[[宮崎日日新聞]]社編、1983年）では「神武さま」として宮崎神宮の大祭が解説されている。</ref>、[[宮崎県]]下最大の祭事として親しまれている。御神幸祭は五穀豊穣を祝う目的も兼ねる。
現在の主催は宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会（宮崎商工会議所）。御神幸祭の観客数は2日間で約15万5,000人（主催者発表、2008年）<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 「神武さま」旅終える 宮崎市] [[宮崎日日新聞]]、2008年11月3日。</ref>。英語表記は（2008年の場合）'''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''となる。
== 歴史 == === 黎明期 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|宮崎神宮御神幸列圖（1940年）]] 起源は資料が散逸しており定かではないが、1785年（[[天明]]4年）の吉田家への「調書草稿」には御神幸祭の記述がある<ref>『養正』第130号2頁。</ref>。また、1876年から残る宮崎神宮の日誌にも御神幸祭が行われていた記述があり、主催者側では1876年を宮崎神宮大祭が始まった年としている<ref>『神武さま まるわかりガイド』1頁。</ref>。当時は宮崎神宮周辺を練り歩くもので現在ほど盛大なものではなかった。
1880年1月4日に現在の宮崎市中村町の[[氏神|氏子]]が「渡御の儀の願い」を提出し、旧暦の3月15日から4月9日までの25日にわたって中村町に御駐輦（[[神輿]]が滞在すること）するようになった。このときに御[[鳳輦]]（ごほうれん、神輿のこと）が[[大淀川]]を越える<ref>宮崎神宮は大淀川の左岸（北）、中村町は右岸（南）に位置する。</ref>ようになった。
1909年に御鳳輦が新調されたのをきっかけに現行のように隊列を組むようになる。規模拡大の背景として宮崎神宮は「境内整備による住民の意識昂揚」を挙げている<ref>『養正』第130号3頁。</ref>。大正から戦前までの「神武さま」は26日例祭・27日[[御旅所|お旅所]]御駐輦（中村<ref>[[宮交シティ]]付近。</ref>）、28日お旅所御駐輦（瀬頭<ref>大淀大橋の北側（大淀川左岸）にある地区。現在の県道341号沿い。</ref>）、29日御還幸と4日間の日程であり、数度の中止（皇族の崩御に伴うもの）を除いて1943年まで続けられた。1940年は[[神武天皇即位紀元|紀元]]2600年（[[紀元二千六百年記念行事]]も参照）ということもあり盛大に催行されたものの、戦局の悪化に伴い1944年には神輿渡御が1日に短縮。1945年は例祭のみ挙行され御神幸祭は中止となった。
=== 戦後の発展期 === 1946年から御神幸祭が再開。しかし、[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部|進駐軍]] (GHQ) による[[神道指令]]や[[日本国憲法]]（[[日本国憲法第20条|第20条]]・[[日本国憲法第89条|第89条]]）に掲げられた[[政教分離原則|政教分離]]の弊害により、新たな問題として「御神幸祭の費用、日程、御旅所の選定」が浮上した。戦前は容易であった公費負担が困難になったためだが、金銭面については1950年に宮崎商工会議所が主体となった「宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会」が設立され解決された。御旅所については1965年から中村（偶数年）・瀬頭（奇数年）の隔年開催となり、日程も1971年から例祭日が10月26日、御神幸祭が例祭後最初の土曜日・日曜日と定められた。
御神幸行列に続く神賑行列（2002年までは混合）には1940年から[[流鏑馬]]武者行列、1949年からはミスシャンシャン馬が加わるようになり、後には「日向ひょっとこ踊り」「神武こども太鼓」を始め様々な舞踊（この中には沖縄の舞踊である[[エイサー]]もあった）が披露されるようになった。1970年代に御神幸行列は最盛期を迎え、1976年には2日間で約50万人の観客を動員した<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 12頁。</ref>。この頃の御神幸行列は[[博多どんたく]]・[[長崎くんち]]とともに'''九州三大祭り'''と称されていた<ref>『宮崎市史 続編』『ぼくらの宮崎県』（[[ポプラ社]]、1979年 ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1）による。</ref>。
=== マンネリ化とその対策 === 1960年代以降「なんでもありのマンネリ化」や「神賑行列が広告的であること」、「行列が長すぎること」が度々指摘されるようになる<ref>『宮崎日日新聞』 1969年10月28日、1970年10月28日など。</ref>が、1990年代にはこの状況が深刻化し御神幸行列の本来の意義も失いつつあるようになった。1985年（雨天のため1日のみの開催）の観客数は28万人で以前とさほど変わらないが<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 232頁。</ref>、1990年代以降は減少の一途を辿り、1994年には29万人<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 522頁。</ref>、1997年には25万人<ref>『[[朝日新聞]]』 1997年11月6日、宮崎版。</ref>、更に2000年には15万人にまで落ち込んだ<ref>『朝日新聞』 2000年11月7日24頁、宮崎版。</ref>。
[[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Okiyomaru.jpg|thumb|200px|おきよ丸]] この対策として2003年から3か年計画で「神武さま」の改革が実行された<ref>[http://www.miyazaki-cci.or.jp/miyazaki/topics2005/11_jinmu/index.html 初登場「おきよ丸」が華を添えにぎわった宮崎神宮大祭] 宮崎商工会議所公式サイト内。</ref>。初年度（2003年）には「神武さま広場」を[[高千穂通り (宮崎市)|高千穂通り]]（[[宮崎県道25号宮崎停車場線|県道25号]]）に設け<ref>2008年は[[橘通り (宮崎市)|橘通り]]（[[国道220号]]）に場所を移している。</ref>御神幸行列と神賑行列を分離。神賑行列に参加していた団体をこちらに参加させた。更に2005年には[[神武東征]]を参考に再現した「おきよ丸」が完成し、御神幸行列の静寂度を高めた。改革が終了した2005年の観客数は18万人と2000年の数値は上回ったものの、2008年には15万5,000人と従来の数値に戻っている。
== 御神幸祭の内容 == 掲載していない画像については[[:Commons:Category:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008|ウィキメディア・コモンズへのリンク]]または[[#外部リンク]]を参照。 === 御神幸行列 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|獅子舞]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|女稚児]] 「聖」と「静」と形容される。1909年に現行のスタイルとなり、100年ほど経った現在も行列の順番（上記画像を参照）は変わらない。 ; 御獅子 御神幸行列の前半に登場。[[獅子舞]]は大人によるものと子どもによるものがある。かまれると病気にならないという言い伝えがある。 ; 御鳳輦（[[神輿]]） 1909年に新調された。この中に「神武さま」が乗る。 ; 女稚児 地元の女の子による稚児行列。男稚児も存在する。 ; [[流鏑馬]] [[宮崎競馬場]]の建設に伴い一度途絶えていた流鏑馬の習慣が、紀元2600年（1940年）を記念して復活したのを機に行列にも加えられる。衣装は鎌倉時代当時を再現したもの。実際の流鏑馬は4月3日（[[神武天皇祭]]）に宮崎神宮境内で行われる。
=== 神賑行列 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC太鼓]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|日向ひょっとこ踊り]] 「俗」と「動」と形容される。かつては御神幸行列に交じって参加していたが、数々の批判を受け2003年からは一部が「神武さま広場」に移動している。 ; おきよ丸 神武東征を参考に再現し2005年に登場。御神幸行列期間外は宮崎神宮境内に安置されている。 ; 太鼓 '''神武こども太鼓'''と神賑行列の締めを飾る'''JC太鼓'''がある。 ; 宮崎まつり百景 宮崎県内各地の祭りを紹介するために開催。2008年は泰平踊り（[[日南市]]飫肥、往路）・木花相撲踊り（宮崎市[[木花地域自治区|木花地区]]、往路）・椎葉平家まつり（[[椎葉村]]、往路）・鉦踊り（[[野尻町]]、復路）・日向[[ひょっとこ]]踊り（[[日向市]]、復路）が参加した。
==== ミスシャンシャン馬 ==== [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|ミスシャンシャン馬（2008年、往路）]] ミスシャンシャン馬は神賑行列の目玉とされる出し物。
シャンシャン馬は[[鵜戸神宮]]へ参拝する新婚夫婦が乗っていた馬のこと。1940年10月に宮崎市で開催された'''日向建国博覧会'''で、当時の宮崎商工会議所会頭であった[[岩切章太郎]]（後の[[宮崎交通]]社長）の提案によりシャンシャン馬20頭をパレードに出したことが原型となった。
1949年に宮崎交通の協賛で開始された。最初の2年は宮崎市内から「代表」となる花嫁を選出。1951年から宮崎県内の各市からの代表制となった。1971年と1972年は宮崎交通の社員からの選出となったが、1973年に「[[宮交シティ]]開業記念」として宮崎市と周辺の町からの代表制となった（ここまでは宮崎交通が全額負担）。2000年は公募制となり、2001年からは協賛企業の女子社員が「ミスシャンシャン馬」として選出されるようになった。協賛企業は50万円を協賛金として出している<ref>『朝日新聞』 2001年10月1日28頁、宮崎版。</ref>。
=== 神武さま広場 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gagaku.jpg|thumb|200px|雅楽の演奏（神武さま広場メインステージ）]] 御神幸行列改革の一環として2003年に開始。高千穂通り（2008年は橘通り）を会場として、御神幸行列往路の後に開催される。かつて神賑行列に参加していた団体の一部はこちらに登場。通りには出店が立ち並び、各所に設けられたステージでは宮崎県内の[[神楽]]や[[雅楽]]・和太鼓の演奏、大道芸などが行われる。
== 日程 == 例祭は'''本宮祭'''と称され、10月26日の10時から宮崎神宮内で催行される。御神幸行列は往路（宮崎神宮御発輦）が例祭後最初の土曜日に、復路（御旅所御発輦）が日曜日（翌日）の13時から催行される。2003年からは往路の終了後に「神武さま広場」が開催される。雨天時は短縮<ref>過去に1981年・1982年・1985年・2011年など。</ref>となる。
* 例祭 - 10月26日午前10時 * 御神幸行列・往路 - 例祭後最初の土曜日 * 神武さま広場 - 御神幸行列・往路の開催日 * 御神幸行列・復路 - 御神幸行列・往路の翌日（日曜日）
=== 開催日 === 御神幸行列の開催日は「10月26日の例祭後最初の土曜日・日曜日」とされていることから、10月26日が日曜日の場合は1週の間が空くことになる。土曜日の場合は例祭の直後または1週間後となる。
; 御神幸行列の開催日 参考として開催日の後に該当する年を記載する。 * 例祭が日曜日 - 11月1日、11月2日（2003年、2008年） * 例祭が月曜日 - 10月31日、11月1日（1998年、2009年） * 例祭が火曜日 - 10月30日、10月31日（1999年、2004年、2010年） * 例祭が水曜日 - 10月29日、10月30日（2005年、2011年） * 例祭が木曜日 - 10月28日、10月29日（2000年、2006年） * 例祭が金曜日 - 10月27日、10月28日（2001年、2007年） * 例祭が土曜日 - 10月26日、10月27日（2002年）または11月2日、11月3日（1991年）
資料によって御神幸祭が「10月下旬の土曜日・日曜日に開催」と記載されていることがあるが、上記のように御神幸祭の開催が11月にずれ込む年もある。
; 御神幸祭の中止 皇族の崩御や病臥（1912年、1914年、1927年、1988年）、戦乱（1877年、1945年）、宮崎神宮の改装（1899年から1906年）、疫病（2020年、2021年）を理由として御神幸祭が中止されることがある。
=== 御神幸行列の順路 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Segashira Otabisho 2009.JPG|thumb|瀬頭御旅所]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Oyodo Otabisho 2022.jpg|thumb|大淀御旅所（2022年、宮崎銀行大淀支店）]] 往路・復路ともに13時出発、15時半ごろに到着となる。デパート前交差点<ref>橘通りと高千穂通りとの交点。</ref>付近は14時ごろに通過する。
往路は宮崎神宮を出発（御発輦）し、[[宮崎県道44号宮崎高鍋線|県道44号]]、[[橘通り (宮崎市)|橘通り]]（[[国道10号]]・[[国道220号]]）を経由。中村（大淀）が御旅所の年はそのまま直進、県道347号（南宮崎駅停車場線）を[[南宮崎駅]]方面へ進み御旅所到着（従来は宮崎交通本社前であったが本社移転により、宮交シティに変更。2022年は宮崎銀行大淀支店）となる。瀬頭が御旅所の年は宮崎市役所前交差点を左折し旭通り（[[宮崎県道11号宮崎島之内線|県道11号]]）を直進、瀬頭交差点で右折し県道341号（宮崎港宮崎停車場線）を大淀大橋方面へ進み、瀬頭御旅所で到着となる。復路はその反対となる。
== 年表 == * 1785年 - 御神幸祭に関する記述がみられる最古の年。 * 1876年 - 宮崎神宮（当時は宮崎神社）公式の資料に御神幸祭に関する記述のある最古の年。これを根拠として1876年が宮崎神宮大祭の始まった最初の年とされる。 * 1877年 - [[西南戦争]]のため中止。 * 1880年 - 宮崎市中村町の住民が御鳳輦（神輿）の渡御を嘆願。これが許可され御神幸の範囲が拡大される。 * 1899年 - 宮崎神宮（当時は宮崎宮）の社殿改装のため1906年まで中止。 * 1907年 - 宮崎神宮の改装が完了し、御神幸行列が復活。 * 1909年 - 御鳳輦を新調。御神幸行列が現在のスタイルに改められる。 * 1912年 - [[明治天皇]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1913年 - 瀬頭に御旅所が建築され、例祭を含めて4日間の開催となる。 * 1914年 - [[昭憲皇太后]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1927年 - [[大正天皇]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1940年 - 流鏑馬武者行列が登場。 * 1944年 - 御神幸行列を1日に短縮。 * 1945年 - 例祭のみ実施し、御神幸行列を中止。 * 1949年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が登場。宮崎市内から代表者を選出。 * 1950年 - 宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会を設立。宮崎商工会議所が主体となった。 * 1951年 - ミスシャンシャン馬を宮崎県内各市からの代表制とする。この年のみ中央御旅所<ref>現在（1963年以降）の宮崎市庁舎（[[宮崎市役所]]）の位置。当時は[[橘公園通り|橘公園]]。</ref>を設け、御鳳輦はここで2泊した。 * 1957年 - 中村御旅所を休憩地とし、瀬頭で御鳳輦を2泊させる形式に変更。 * 1960年 - 中村・瀬頭でそれぞれ御鳳輦を1泊させる形式に戻す。 * 1965年 - 御旅所が中村（偶数年）・瀬頭（奇数年）の隔年交代となり、御神幸行列も2日に短縮される。 * 1971年 - 日程が現行のもの（26日例祭、御神幸行列は例祭後の土曜日・日曜日）に変更。ミスシャンシャン馬が宮崎交通社員からの代表制となる。 * 1972年 - 御神幸行列初日（土曜日）の夜間に橘通り（国道220号）を[[歩行者天国]]とする。現在の「神武さま広場」の原型。 * 1973年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が宮崎市と周辺の町からの代表制となる。 * 1988年 - [[昭和天皇]]病臥による「自粛」のため中止<ref>『宮崎日日新聞』 1988年9月27日19頁。</ref>。 * 2000年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が公募制になる。 * 2001年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が協賛企業の社員から選出される方式へ変更。 * 2003年 - 「神武さま広場」が高千穂通りに登場。神賑行列の参加者の一部がこちらに登場するようになる。 * 2005年 - おきよ丸が登場。 * 2008年 - 神武さま広場の場所を橘通りに移転。 * 2010年 - 神武さま広場の場所を再び高千穂通りに移転。アマテラスの山車が登場。 * 2014年 - 宮崎市制90周年を記念して「神々のパレード」に山車が登場。 * 2020年・2021年 - 例祭のみ実施し、御神幸行列を中止<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/kennai/_47527.html 神武さま御神幸行列中止 イベントは形式変更]『宮崎日日新聞』2020年9月16日。</ref><ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/kennai/_56888.html 「神武さま」行列、ステージ催し中止]『宮崎日日新聞』2021年9月23日。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史』 1959年3月、846-847頁。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 年表』 1974年3月。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 続編』 1978年3月、1,609-1,612頁。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 1999年3月。 * 宮崎神宮社務所発行 『養正』 爽秋号（第130号）、2008年10月1日。 * 『神武さま まるわかりガイド』（現地配布のパンフレット）、2008年11月。 * 『宮崎日日新聞』 通常記事として例年掲載するほかに、特集として御神幸祭前の金曜日に2ページ立てで紹介される。2008年を例にすると同年10月31日に掲載。
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇祭]] * [[NHK宮崎放送局]] 県道44号沿線に所在。2010年代まで御神幸行列に合わせて局舎公開を行っていた。ただしNHKは10月の最終土曜日・日曜日が局舎公開日であるため、御神幸行列と日程が異なる年もある（2008年など）。
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival}} * [https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/ 宮崎神宮公式ウェブサイト] * [https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html 2008年宮崎神宮大祭] ポータルサイト「miten」（MRT[[宮崎放送]]関連企業「デンサン」運営）内コンテンツ。同年の日程や経路、ミスシャンシャン馬のレポートが公開されている。かつてはFlash Video形式で過去の映像も公開していた。
{{DEFAULTSORT:みやさきしんくうたいさい}} [[Category:宮崎県の祭り]] [[Category:宮崎市の歴史]] [[Category:宮崎市の文化]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[宮崎神宮大祭]] to [[Miyazaki Shrine Festival]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|2008年の御神幸行列。御鳳輦がデパート前交差点を通る。]] '''[[宮崎神宮]]の[[例祭|大祭]]'''（みやざきじんぐうのたいさい）は[[10月26日]]に催行され、例祭後最初の[[土曜日]]・[[日曜日]]に御[[神幸祭]]が[[宮崎市]]内で行われる。
特に御神幸行列と神賑行列から成る御神幸祭は'''神武さま'''（祭神の[[神武天皇]]の意）と称され<ref>『宮崎県大百科事典』（[[宮崎日日新聞]]社編、1983年）では「神武さま」として宮崎神宮の大祭が解説されている。</ref>、[[宮崎県]]下最大の祭事として親しまれている。御神幸祭は五穀豊穣を祝う目的も兼ねる。
現在の主催は宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会（宮崎商工会議所）。御神幸祭の観客数は2日間で約15万5,000人（主催者発表、2008年）<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 「神武さま」旅終える 宮崎市] [[宮崎日日新聞]]、2008年11月3日。</ref>。英語表記は（2008年の場合）'''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''となる。
== 歴史 == === 黎明期 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|宮崎神宮御神幸列圖（1940年）]] 起源は資料が散逸しており定かではないが、1785年（[[天明]]4年）の吉田家への「調書草稿」には御神幸祭の記述がある<ref>『養正』第130号2頁。</ref>。また、1876年から残る宮崎神宮の日誌にも御神幸祭が行われていた記述があり、主催者側では1876年を宮崎神宮大祭が始まった年としている<ref>『神武さま まるわかりガイド』1頁。</ref>。当時は宮崎神宮周辺を練り歩くもので現在ほど盛大なものではなかった。
1880年1月4日に現在の宮崎市中村町の[[氏神|氏子]]が「渡御の儀の願い」を提出し、旧暦の3月15日から4月9日までの25日にわたって中村町に御駐輦（[[神輿]]が滞在すること）するようになった。このときに御[[鳳輦]]（ごほうれん、神輿のこと）が[[大淀川]]を越える<ref>宮崎神宮は大淀川の左岸（北）、中村町は右岸（南）に位置する。</ref>ようになった。
1909年に御鳳輦が新調されたのをきっかけに現行のように隊列を組むようになる。規模拡大の背景として宮崎神宮は「境内整備による住民の意識昂揚」を挙げている<ref>『養正』第130号3頁。</ref>。大正から戦前までの「神武さま」は26日例祭・27日[[御旅所|お旅所]]御駐輦（中村<ref>[[宮交シティ]]付近。</ref>）、28日お旅所御駐輦（瀬頭<ref>大淀大橋の北側（大淀川左岸）にある地区。現在の県道341号沿い。</ref>）、29日御還幸と4日間の日程であり、数度の中止（皇族の崩御に伴うもの）を除いて1943年まで続けられた。1940年は[[神武天皇即位紀元|紀元]]2600年（[[紀元二千六百年記念行事]]も参照）ということもあり盛大に催行されたものの、戦局の悪化に伴い1944年には神輿渡御が1日に短縮。1945年は例祭のみ挙行され御神幸祭は中止となった。
=== 戦後の発展期 === 1946年から御神幸祭が再開。しかし、[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部|進駐軍]] (GHQ) による[[神道指令]]や[[日本国憲法]]（[[日本国憲法第20条|第20条]]・[[日本国憲法第89条|第89条]]）に掲げられた[[政教分離原則|政教分離]]の弊害により、新たな問題として「御神幸祭の費用、日程、御旅所の選定」が浮上した。戦前は容易であった公費負担が困難になったためだが、金銭面については1950年に宮崎商工会議所が主体となった「宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会」が設立され解決された。御旅所については1965年から中村（偶数年）・瀬頭（奇数年）の隔年開催となり、日程も1971年から例祭日が10月26日、御神幸祭が例祭後最初の土曜日・日曜日と定められた。
御神幸行列に続く神賑行列（2002年までは混合）には1940年から[[流鏑馬]]武者行列、1949年からはミスシャンシャン馬が加わるようになり、後には「日向ひょっとこ踊り」「神武こども太鼓」を始め様々な舞踊（この中には沖縄の舞踊である[[エイサー]]もあった）が披露されるようになった。1970年代に御神幸行列は最盛期を迎え、1976年には2日間で約50万人の観客を動員した<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 12頁。</ref>。この頃の御神幸行列は[[博多どんたく]]・[[長崎くんち]]とともに'''九州三大祭り'''と称されていた<ref>『宮崎市史 続編』『ぼくらの宮崎県』（[[ポプラ社]]、1979年 ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1）による。</ref>。
=== マンネリ化とその対策 === 1960年代以降「なんでもありのマンネリ化」や「神賑行列が広告的であること」、「行列が長すぎること」が度々指摘されるようになる<ref>『宮崎日日新聞』 1969年10月28日、1970年10月28日など。</ref>が、1990年代にはこの状況が深刻化し御神幸行列の本来の意義も失いつつあるようになった。1985年（雨天のため1日のみの開催）の観客数は28万人で以前とさほど変わらないが<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 232頁。</ref>、1990年代以降は減少の一途を辿り、1994年には29万人<ref>『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 522頁。</ref>、1997年には25万人<ref>『[[朝日新聞]]』 1997年11月6日、宮崎版。</ref>、更に2000年には15万人にまで落ち込んだ<ref>『朝日新聞』 2000年11月7日24頁、宮崎版。</ref>。
[[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Okiyomaru.jpg|thumb|200px|おきよ丸]] この対策として2003年から3か年計画で「神武さま」の改革が実行された<ref>[http://www.miyazaki-cci.or.jp/miyazaki/topics2005/11_jinmu/index.html 初登場「おきよ丸」が華を添えにぎわった宮崎神宮大祭] 宮崎商工会議所公式サイト内。</ref>。初年度（2003年）には「神武さま広場」を[[高千穂通り (宮崎市)|高千穂通り]]（[[宮崎県道25号宮崎停車場線|県道25号]]）に設け<ref>2008年は[[橘通り (宮崎市)|橘通り]]（[[国道220号]]）に場所を移している。</ref>御神幸行列と神賑行列を分離。神賑行列に参加していた団体をこちらに参加させた。更に2005年には[[神武東征]]を参考に再現した「おきよ丸」が完成し、御神幸行列の静寂度を高めた。改革が終了した2005年の観客数は18万人と2000年の数値は上回ったものの、2008年には15万5,000人と従来の数値に戻っている。
== 御神幸祭の内容 == 掲載していない画像については[[:Commons:Category:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008|ウィキメディア・コモンズへのリンク]]または[[#外部リンク]]を参照。 === 御神幸行列 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|獅子舞]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|女稚児]] 「聖」と「静」と形容される。1909年に現行のスタイルとなり、100年ほど経った現在も行列の順番（上記画像を参照）は変わらない。 ; 御獅子 御神幸行列の前半に登場。[[獅子舞]]は大人によるものと子どもによるものがある。かまれると病気にならないという言い伝えがある。 ; 御鳳輦（[[神輿]]） 1909年に新調された。この中に「神武さま」が乗る。 ; 女稚児 地元の女の子による稚児行列。男稚児も存在する。 ; [[流鏑馬]] [[宮崎競馬場]]の建設に伴い一度途絶えていた流鏑馬の習慣が、紀元2600年（1940年）を記念して復活したのを機に行列にも加えられる。衣装は鎌倉時代当時を再現したもの。実際の流鏑馬は4月3日（[[神武天皇祭]]）に宮崎神宮境内で行われる。
=== 神賑行列 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC太鼓]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|日向ひょっとこ踊り]] 「俗」と「動」と形容される。かつては御神幸行列に交じって参加していたが、数々の批判を受け2003年からは一部が「神武さま広場」に移動している。 ; おきよ丸 神武東征を参考に再現し2005年に登場。御神幸行列期間外は宮崎神宮境内に安置されている。 ; 太鼓 '''神武こども太鼓'''と神賑行列の締めを飾る'''JC太鼓'''がある。 ; 宮崎まつり百景 宮崎県内各地の祭りを紹介するために開催。2008年は泰平踊り（[[日南市]]飫肥、往路）・木花相撲踊り（宮崎市[[木花地域自治区|木花地区]]、往路）・椎葉平家まつり（[[椎葉村]]、往路）・鉦踊り（[[野尻町]]、復路）・日向[[ひょっとこ]]踊り（[[日向市]]、復路）が参加した。
==== ミスシャンシャン馬 ==== [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|ミスシャンシャン馬（2008年、往路）]] ミスシャンシャン馬は神賑行列の目玉とされる出し物。
シャンシャン馬は[[鵜戸神宮]]へ参拝する新婚夫婦が乗っていた馬のこと。1940年10月に宮崎市で開催された'''日向建国博覧会'''で、当時の宮崎商工会議所会頭であった[[岩切章太郎]]（後の[[宮崎交通]]社長）の提案によりシャンシャン馬20頭をパレードに出したことが原型となった。
1949年に宮崎交通の協賛で開始された。最初の2年は宮崎市内から「代表」となる花嫁を選出。1951年から宮崎県内の各市からの代表制となった。1971年と1972年は宮崎交通の社員からの選出となったが、1973年に「[[宮交シティ]]開業記念」として宮崎市と周辺の町からの代表制となった（ここまでは宮崎交通が全額負担）。2000年は公募制となり、2001年からは協賛企業の女子社員が「ミスシャンシャン馬」として選出されるようになった。協賛企業は50万円を協賛金として出している<ref>『朝日新聞』 2001年10月1日28頁、宮崎版。</ref>。
=== 神武さま広場 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gagaku.jpg|thumb|200px|雅楽の演奏（神武さま広場メインステージ）]] 御神幸行列改革の一環として2003年に開始。高千穂通り（2008年は橘通り）を会場として、御神幸行列往路の後に開催される。かつて神賑行列に参加していた団体の一部はこちらに登場。通りには出店が立ち並び、各所に設けられたステージでは宮崎県内の[[神楽]]や[[雅楽]]・和太鼓の演奏、大道芸などが行われる。
== 日程 == 例祭は'''本宮祭'''と称され、10月26日の10時から宮崎神宮内で催行される。御神幸行列は往路（宮崎神宮御発輦）が例祭後最初の土曜日に、復路（御旅所御発輦）が日曜日（翌日）の13時から催行される。2003年からは往路の終了後に「神武さま広場」が開催される。雨天時は短縮<ref>過去に1981年・1982年・1985年・2011年など。</ref>となる。
* 例祭 - 10月26日午前10時 * 御神幸行列・往路 - 例祭後最初の土曜日 * 神武さま広場 - 御神幸行列・往路の開催日 * 御神幸行列・復路 - 御神幸行列・往路の翌日（日曜日）
=== 開催日 === 御神幸行列の開催日は「10月26日の例祭後最初の土曜日・日曜日」とされていることから、10月26日が日曜日の場合は1週の間が空くことになる。土曜日の場合は例祭の直後または1週間後となる。
; 御神幸行列の開催日 参考として開催日の後に該当する年を記載する。 * 例祭が日曜日 - 11月1日、11月2日（2003年、2008年） * 例祭が月曜日 - 10月31日、11月1日（1998年、2009年） * 例祭が火曜日 - 10月30日、10月31日（1999年、2004年、2010年） * 例祭が水曜日 - 10月29日、10月30日（2005年、2011年） * 例祭が木曜日 - 10月28日、10月29日（2000年、2006年） * 例祭が金曜日 - 10月27日、10月28日（2001年、2007年） * 例祭が土曜日 - 10月26日、10月27日（2002年）または11月2日、11月3日（1991年）
資料によって御神幸祭が「10月下旬の土曜日・日曜日に開催」と記載されていることがあるが、上記のように御神幸祭の開催が11月にずれ込む年もある。
; 御神幸祭の中止 皇族の崩御や病臥（1912年、1914年、1927年、1988年）、戦乱（1877年、1945年）、宮崎神宮の改装（1899年から1906年）、疫病（2020年、2021年）を理由として御神幸祭が中止されることがある。
=== 御神幸行列の順路 === [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Segashira Otabisho 2009.JPG|thumb|瀬頭御旅所]] [[ファイル:Miyazaki Shrine Oyodo Otabisho 2022.jpg|thumb|大淀御旅所（2022年、宮崎銀行大淀支店）]] 往路・復路ともに13時出発、15時半ごろに到着となる。デパート前交差点<ref>橘通りと高千穂通りとの交点。</ref>付近は14時ごろに通過する。
往路は宮崎神宮を出発（御発輦）し、[[宮崎県道44号宮崎高鍋線|県道44号]]、[[橘通り (宮崎市)|橘通り]]（[[国道10号]]・[[国道220号]]）を経由。中村（大淀）が御旅所の年はそのまま直進、県道347号（南宮崎駅停車場線）を[[南宮崎駅]]方面へ進み御旅所到着（従来は宮崎交通本社前であったが本社移転により、宮交シティに変更。2022年は宮崎銀行大淀支店）となる。瀬頭が御旅所の年は宮崎市役所前交差点を左折し旭通り（[[宮崎県道11号宮崎島之内線|県道11号]]）を直進、瀬頭交差点で右折し県道341号（宮崎港宮崎停車場線）を大淀大橋方面へ進み、瀬頭御旅所で到着となる。復路はその反対となる。
== 年表 == * 1785年 - 御神幸祭に関する記述がみられる最古の年。 * 1876年 - 宮崎神宮（当時は宮崎神社）公式の資料に御神幸祭に関する記述のある最古の年。これを根拠として1876年が宮崎神宮大祭の始まった最初の年とされる。 * 1877年 - [[西南戦争]]のため中止。 * 1880年 - 宮崎市中村町の住民が御鳳輦（神輿）の渡御を嘆願。これが許可され御神幸の範囲が拡大される。 * 1899年 - 宮崎神宮（当時は宮崎宮）の社殿改装のため1906年まで中止。 * 1907年 - 宮崎神宮の改装が完了し、御神幸行列が復活。 * 1909年 - 御鳳輦を新調。御神幸行列が現在のスタイルに改められる。 * 1912年 - [[明治天皇]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1913年 - 瀬頭に御旅所が建築され、例祭を含めて4日間の開催となる。 * 1914年 - [[昭憲皇太后]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1927年 - [[大正天皇]]の崩御に伴い中止。 * 1940年 - 流鏑馬武者行列が登場。 * 1944年 - 御神幸行列を1日に短縮。 * 1945年 - 例祭のみ実施し、御神幸行列を中止。 * 1949年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が登場。宮崎市内から代表者を選出。 * 1950年 - 宮崎神宮御神幸祭奉賛会を設立。宮崎商工会議所が主体となった。 * 1951年 - ミスシャンシャン馬を宮崎県内各市からの代表制とする。この年のみ中央御旅所<ref>現在（1963年以降）の宮崎市庁舎（[[宮崎市役所]]）の位置。当時は[[橘公園通り|橘公園]]。</ref>を設け、御鳳輦はここで2泊した。 * 1957年 - 中村御旅所を休憩地とし、瀬頭で御鳳輦を2泊させる形式に変更。 * 1960年 - 中村・瀬頭でそれぞれ御鳳輦を1泊させる形式に戻す。 * 1965年 - 御旅所が中村（偶数年）・瀬頭（奇数年）の隔年交代となり、御神幸行列も2日に短縮される。 * 1971年 - 日程が現行のもの（26日例祭、御神幸行列は例祭後の土曜日・日曜日）に変更。ミスシャンシャン馬が宮崎交通社員からの代表制となる。 * 1972年 - 御神幸行列初日（土曜日）の夜間に橘通り（国道220号）を[[歩行者天国]]とする。現在の「神武さま広場」の原型。 * 1973年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が宮崎市と周辺の町からの代表制となる。 * 1988年 - [[昭和天皇]]病臥による「自粛」のため中止<ref>『宮崎日日新聞』 1988年9月27日19頁。</ref>。 * 2000年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が公募制になる。 * 2001年 - ミスシャンシャン馬が協賛企業の社員から選出される方式へ変更。 * 2003年 - 「神武さま広場」が高千穂通りに登場。神賑行列の参加者の一部がこちらに登場するようになる。 * 2005年 - おきよ丸が登場。 * 2008年 - 神武さま広場の場所を橘通りに移転。 * 2010年 - 神武さま広場の場所を再び高千穂通りに移転。アマテラスの山車が登場。 * 2014年 - 宮崎市制90周年を記念して「神々のパレード」に山車が登場。 * 2020年・2021年 - 例祭のみ実施し、御神幸行列を中止<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/kennai/_47527.html 神武さま御神幸行列中止 イベントは形式変更]『宮崎日日新聞』2020年9月16日。</ref><ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/kennai/_56888.html 「神武さま」行列、ステージ催し中止]『宮崎日日新聞』2021年9月23日。</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史』 1959年3月、846-847頁。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 年表』 1974年3月。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 続編』 1978年3月、1,609-1,612頁。 * 宮崎市史編さん委員会・編 『宮崎市史 年表 続編』 1999年3月。 * 宮崎神宮社務所発行 『養正』 爽秋号（第130号）、2008年10月1日。 * 『神武さま まるわかりガイド』（現地配布のパンフレット）、2008年11月。 * 『宮崎日日新聞』 通常記事として例年掲載するほかに、特集として御神幸祭前の金曜日に2ページ立てで紹介される。2008年を例にすると同年10月31日に掲載。
== 関連項目 == *[[神武天皇祭]] * [[NHK宮崎放送局]] 県道44号沿線に所在。2010年代まで御神幸行列に合わせて局舎公開を行っていた。ただしNHKは10月の最終土曜日・日曜日が局舎公開日であるため、御神幸行列と日程が異なる年もある（2008年など）。
== 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival}} * [https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/ 宮崎神宮公式ウェブサイト] * [https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html 2008年宮崎神宮大祭] ポータルサイト「miten」（MRT[[宮崎放送]]関連企業「デンサン」運営）内コンテンツ。同年の日程や経路、ミスシャンシャン馬のレポートが公開されている。かつてはFlash Video形式で過去の映像も公開していた。
{{DEFAULTSORT:みやさきしんくうたいさい}} [[Category:宮崎県の祭り]] [[Category:宮崎市の歴史]] [[Category:宮崎市の文化]]

revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

}}

This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

revision.comment: Bot: cleanup/reformat
revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)
{{translated page|ja|宮崎神宮大祭|version=99676427|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)
{{translated page|ja|宮崎神宮大祭|version=99676427|comment=Imported full ja history}}

In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

<!--Miyazaki Shrine Festival--><!--Miyazaki Shrine Festival--> [[Category:宮崎市の文化]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Festivals of Miyazaki prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival}}

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Culture of Miyazaki City
revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)

[[sn:Miyazaki Shrine Festival]] [[ja:宮崎神宮大祭]]
<!--Miyazaki Shrine Festival--><!--Miyazaki Shrine Festival--> [[Category:宮崎市の文化]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Festivals of Miyazaki prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
[[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Gohouren 01.jpg|thumb|250px|The 2008 Goshinkō procession passing a department store intersection.]] The '''Grand Festival of Miyazaki Shrine''' (宮崎神宮の大祭, Miyazaki Jingū no Taisai) is held annually on [[October 26]], followed by the [[Goshinkō Festival]] during the first [[Saturday]] and [[Sunday]] after the grand festival. The Goshinkō Festival takes place within [[Miyazaki City]], featuring two processions: the '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the '''Shinnigi Procession'''. It is affectionately known as "Jinmu-sama," referring to its deity, [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]]<ref>‘Miyazaki Prefectural Encyclopedia’ (宮崎県大百科事典), Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, describes the festival as "Jinmu-sama."</ref> and is celebrated as the largest festival in [[Miyazaki Prefecture]]. The festival also serves as a thanksgiving event for an abundant harvest.
The festival is currently organized by the Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee, led by the [[Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce and Industry]]. Approximately 155,000 people attended the 2008 festival over its two days (according to organizers). Its English title for that year was the '''Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008'''<ref>[https://www.the-miyanichi.co.jp/contents/index.php?itemid=12274 "Jinmu-sama" completes its journey in Miyazaki City], Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, November 3, 2008.</ref>.
== History== === Early Days=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Illustration 1940.jpg|thumb|250px|Illustration of the Goshinkō Procession (1940)]] The exact origins of the festival are unclear due to lost records, but references to the Goshinkō Festival can be found as early as 1785 in documents submitted to the Yoshida family<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 2.</ref>. The Miyazaki Shrine diaries dating back to 1876 also mention the festival, and the organizers recognize this year as the official start of the Grand Festival<ref>‘Jinmu-sama Maruwakari Guide,’ p. 1.</ref>. Initially, the procession was modest, confined to the area around the shrine.
In 1880, the shrine parishioners of Nakamura Town (now part of Miyazaki City) petitioned for a ritual to extend the procession across the [[Ōyodo River]]. Permission was granted, and the procession stayed in Nakamura for 25 days from the 15th day of the third lunar month. This marked the beginning of the crossing of the Ōyodo River<ref>Miyazaki Shrine is located on the northern (left) bank, while Nakamura is on the southern (right) bank.</ref>.
In 1909, a new sacred palanquin ([[mikoshi]]) was created, and the procession's structure was formalized. This change reflected the shrine's emphasis on community engagement and the expansion of festival activities<ref>‘Yōsei,’ Issue 130, p. 3.</ref>. From the [[Taishō period]] to the pre-war years, the festival spanned four days and was held regularly, except during periods of mourning for the Imperial Family. In 1940, the festival was celebrated on a grand scale to mark the 2600th year since Emperor Jimmu's accession. However, the worsening war situation led to a scaled-down event in 1944 and complete cancellation in 1945.
=== Post-war Revival=== The festival resumed in 1946, but new challenges arose due to the [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[:en:Allied Occupation of Japan|Occupation authorities]] and the [[Constitution of Japan]]'s [[Separation of church and state|separation of religion and state]]. Public funding became difficult, prompting the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce to establish the "Miyazaki Jingū Goshinkō Festival Support Committee" in 1950 to address financial issues. By 1965, the festival adopted a biennial rotation for its destinations (Nakamura in even years and Segashira in odd years) and a two-day schedule, solidifying its modern format in 1971.
The Goshinkō Procession reached its peak in the 1970s, drawing approximately 500,000 spectators in 1976<ref>‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement),’ p. 12.</ref>. It was once regarded as one of Kyushu's three major festivals alongside [[Hakata Dontaku]] and [[Nagasaki Kunchi]]<ref>Based on references from ‘Chronology of Miyazaki City (Supplement)’ and ‘Our Miyazaki Prefecture,’ Poplar Publishing, 1979, ISBN 978-4-591-01443-1.</ref>.
=== Modern Challenges and Reforms=== By the 1990s, the festival faced criticism for becoming overly commercialized and losing its traditional focus<ref>Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun articles, October 28, 1969, and October 28, 1970.</ref>. Attendance dropped significantly, from 280,000 in 1985 to 150,000 in 2000<ref>Asahi Shimbun, November 7, 2000, Miyazaki edition.</ref>.
In 2003, reforms began, including the introduction of the "Jinmu-sama Plaza" on [[Takachiho Avenue]] and separating the Goshinkō and Shinnigi processions. By 2005, the festival added the "Okiyo-maru," a boat model inspired by Emperor Jimmu's mythical eastern expedition. While these reforms briefly increased attendance to 180,000, numbers fell back to pre-reform levels by 2008.
== Goshinkō Festival Details== The festival consists of two main processions: the solemn and sacred '''Goshinkō Procession''' and the lively and entertaining '''Shinnigi Procession.'''
=== Goshinkō Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Lion Dance 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Lion Dance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Female Chigo 01.jpg|thumb|200px|Female Chigo participants]] The Goshinkō Procession is described as "sacred and serene," with its current style established in 1909. Notable elements include: * '''Lion Dances''': Performed by both children and adults. It is said that being bitten by the lion wards off illness. * '''Sacred Palanquin (Gohōren)''': A 1909 creation, carrying "Jinmu-sama." * '''Child Attendants (Chigo)''': Featuring local boys and girls in traditional attire. * '''Mounted Archers ([[Yabusame]])''': Inspired by Kamakura-period costumes, these riders also perform in April during the [[Emperor Jimmu Festival]].
=== Shinnigi Procession=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 JC Taiko 01.jpg|thumb|200px|JC Taiko performance]] [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Hyottoko.jpg|thumb|200px|Hyottoko Dance from Hyuga]] The Shinnigi Procession, characterized as "lively and dynamic," includes performances such as: * '''Okiyo-maru''': A highlight of the Goshinkō Procession, displayed at Miyazaki Shrine when not in use. * '''Drumming''': Including the "Jinmu Children's Drumming" and the concluding "JC Taiko." * '''Regional Festivals''': Performances representing various festivals across Miyazaki Prefecture, such as Hyuga's Hyottoko Dance and Obi's Taihei Dance.
===Miss Shanshan Horses=== [[File:Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival in 2008 Miss Shanshan Uma JONF.jpg|thumb|200px|Miss Shanshan Horse (2008)]] "Miss Shanshan" is a key attraction, inspired by the historical practice of newlywed couples riding horses to [[Udo Shrine]]. This tradition began in 1940 during the Hyuga Nation-Building Expo and evolved into a public competition supported by the Miyazaki Chamber of Commerce. Today, corporate sponsors nominate representatives to participate.
=== Jinmu-sama Plaza=== The plaza serves as a cultural showcase with performances of [[Kagura]], [[Gagaku]], and other traditional arts. Stalls line the streets, creating a festive atmosphere.
== Schedule== The Grand Festival (Hongū Festival) is held at Miyazaki Shrine on October 26 at 10:00 AM. The Goshinkō Procession takes place on the following Saturday and Sunday, with the Jinmu-sama Plaza held after the Saturday procession.
* '''Grand Festival''': October 26, 10:00 AM * '''Goshinkō Procession (Outbound)''': First Saturday after October 26 * '''Jinmu-sama Plaza''': Held on the same day as the outbound procession * '''Goshinkō Procession (Return)''': Following Sunday
=== Procession Route=== The outbound procession begins at 1:00 PM, departing Miyazaki Shrine and traveling via [[Route 10]] to Nakamura (even years) or Segashira (odd years). It concludes around 3:30 PM. The return procession follows the reverse route.
== References== {{reflist}}
== Related Links== - [Miyazaki Shrine Official Website](https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/) - [2008 Miyazaki Shrine Grand Festival Archive](https://www.miten.jp/miten/modules/addon_sp/index.php/200810_jinmusama_wide/frame.html) (Includes reports and images)
